text: Root of inferior mesenteric artery bleeding during robotic D, three lymph node dissection for rectal cancer, a video vignette.
This is a seventy-one-year-old man who presented with hematochesia for three months.
Coloscopy showed a mass in upper rectum.
Biopsy revealed a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma.
No distant metastasis.
The operation was performed with the patient under in a supine position with his legs apart.

The operating table is positioned in Trendelenburg with a thirty-degree angle and a right lateral inclination of twenty-five degrees. The surgical setup includes eight-millimeter trocars and one twelve-millimeter assistant trocar.
The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon from the left abdominal wall, detaching it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid mesentery using a medial approach, manipulating the tissue to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon ligates the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin, securing the vessel with visible ligatures.
The surgeon uses a permanent cautery hook to dissect the skeletonized inferior mesenteric artery, although the dissection is noted to be improperly performed.
A vascular avulsion is observed at the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, indicating a separation of the vessel from its attachment point.
The surgeon applies compression hemostasis using fenestrated bipolar forceps to control bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon replaces the permanent cautery hook with a needle driver, preparing for the next step in the procedure.

The surgeon performs a figure-eight suture combined with a running suture on the bleeding site using a 4-0 Prolene suture, addressing the vascular avulsion at the root of the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon reinforces the bleeding site with an additional 4-0 Prolene suture, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical area.
The surgical field shows no active bleeding, indicating successful hemostasis following previous interventions.
Then, TME and end-to-end colorectal anastomosis were performed conventionally.
The operation time was three hours and twenty minutes.
The estimated blood loss was one hundred milliliters.
The patient was discharged on postoperative day seven.
There are some tips for the procedure.
Tip one, permanent cautery hook should not be used for the transection of major vessels because of the potential thermal conduction.
Tip two, in the situation of IMA root injury, use the compression to control the bleeding immediately.
Tip three, robotic repair of major vascular injuries with proline suturing could be attempted by experienced surgeons.
In conclusion, bleeding at root of IMA may be managed safely in robotic setting, thereby avoiding conversion to laparotomy.
Thank you for your attention.
